Trend Summary:
The September 16 release cycle showed a net increase of 17 (39 opened vs 22 solved). While this represents solid delivery output, the volume of new issues—particularly the 110 Zendesk tickets opened versus 54 resolved—signals heightened post-release customer impact that requires continued focus.
Content, Curation, and Opportunities (CCO) emerged as a positive area, both solving the most bugs (5) and maintaining active development momentum. The team addressed critical issues around file processing, content updates via API, and pathway management, demonstrating consistent progress in core functionality areas. Academy-related fixes also showed positive momentum with 4 resolved issues addressing project submissions, progress tracking, and admin permissions—critical areas for user experience.
Skills and Academies functionality generated elevated concern, with Skills-related bugs increasing substantially (5 new issues including Skills+ publishing problems and skill standard assignment failures) and Academies experiencing critical duplication and syncing issues. The concentration of Academy bugs around duplicate functionality and completion tracking suggests systemic architectural challenges requiring deeper investigation.
SLA performance remains critically challenged with all P2 Major bugs exceeding their 30-day target and only 69% of P3/P4 bugs meeting the 120-day SLA. One particularly concerning P3 bug (PD-102497) remained open for 422 days before resolution, highlighting ongoing technical debt that continues to impact delivery timelines and customer satisfaction.

Trend Summary:
Significant backlog accumulation this cycle. We opened 32 new bugs while solving only 23 (21 + 2 ACM), resulting in 9 net new bugs added to the backlog. The ratio of bugs opened to solved indicates the release introduced new issues or surfaced existing problems at a rate faster than the team's resolution capacity.
Mobile platform issues are gaining attention. Multiple bugs were reported around the Android/iOS mobile app experience, including video playback failures in pathways, display language inconsistencies showing French (Canada) instead of French, and connection errors with Maestro options. This concentration of mobile bugs suggests the mobile experience requires focused stabilization work.
Strong resolution of automation and reporting issues. The team successfully addressed several critical automation bugs affecting user group assignments, assigned learning displays, and trigger configurations. Reporting fixes included resolving dynamic admin group permissions and pathway follower report errors, improving administrative functionality for (tech) admin users.
Academy platform showing healthy progress but limited scope. Only 2 Academy bugs were resolved in the specified timeframe, addressing critical search visibility and video rendering issues. However, new academy-related bugs continue to emerge around session timeouts, automation emails, bulk nudge functionality, and help widget display, suggesting ongoing platform maturation challenges.
Skills+ feature continues maturation process. Multiple bugs both opened and closed relate to Skills+ functionality, including skill scale changes, suggestion workflows, bulk approval processes, and modal displays. The ongoing attention to this newer feature area is expected as it stabilizes with real-world usage patterns.

Trend Summary:
Significant bug backlog growth: This release saw 39 new bugs opened compared to only 17 bugs resolved (12 from the main release + 5 from ACM Content Marketplace team), resulting in a net increase of 22 bugs in the backlog. This  ratio of bugs opened to closed is a concerning trend that suggests the release may have introduced new issues or exposed existing problems that require immediate attention to prevent further backlog accumulation.
Content Marketplace team contribution: The ACM Content Marketplace team resolved 5 critical bugs during the two-week cycle, including academy member upload issues, project submission problems, completion syncing issues, and session timeout errors. These fixes addressed high-impact issues affecting multiple enterprise accounts and accounted for 18 Zendesk tickets, demonstrating strong focus on customer-reported problems.
Skills and integration challenges persist: Multiple bugs were logged related to Skills features (skill profiles, ratings, assignments) and various integrations (SuccessFactors, Pluralsight, CSV collectors). The opened bugs span across Discovery pages, API issues, file upload problems, and Skills management, indicating that recent feature development or platform changes may have introduced regressions that need systematic investigation.
SLA performance requires attention: Only 12 of 17 bugs (71%) were resolved within SLA targets. One P2 Major bug exceeded its 30-day SLA by 25 days (taking 55 days total), and four P3 Minor bugs significantly exceeded the 120-day threshold (ranging from 137-201 days). These older bugs represent technical debt from March through May that should be prioritized to maintain service commitments and customer trust.

Trend Summary:
Challenging cycle with significantly more bugs opened than solved. With 41 bugs reported versus only 24 resolved (21 LXP + 3 ACM), we're seeing a net increase of 17 bugs in the backlog. This ratio of opened-to-solved indicates the release may have introduced instability or that client-reporting velocity has increased substantially.
Integration and catalog-related issues dominated both opened and solved bugs this cycle. Notably, multiple third-party integrations experienced failures (Workday, GetAbstract, Coursera, Docebo, GenericCSV/RSS), suggesting potential systemic issues with the integration framework or recent changes affecting collector reliability. Reporting and Skills functionality issues remain persistent themes, with multiple bugs related to data display, skill ratings, and report accuracy. This pattern has appeared in previous cycles.
Positive note: SLA compliance at 67% shows the team is resolving most bugs within reasonable timeframes, with P3 Minor bugs performing particularly well at 86% compliance (12 of 14). The quick resolution times for bugs within SLA (6-14 days for P2, 0-75 days for P3) demonstrate strong execution when issues are prioritized.
Critical concern: P2 Major bug SLA compliance is only 40% (4 of 10), with critical issues taking 38-101 days to resolve when they exceed SLA. This suggests either improper priority assignment or resource constraints preventing timely resolution of high-priority client-impacting issues.

Trend Summary:
Massive bug influx indicates significant release stability issues. With 68 bugs opened versus only 26 resolved (24 LXP + 2 ACM), this release produced a net increase of +42 bugs. This dramatic spike suggests the November 11th deployment introduced substantial new defects across multiple system areas.
Localization failures dominated new issues, with 9 bugs affecting internationalization including broken Spanish translations, null language codes in xAPI statements, and incorrect decimal separators in French/French Canadian. Skills+ translation problems were particularly prevalent, impacting pathway displays and skill filter functionality across non-English interfaces.
Post-deployment production incidents required immediate attention, notably ElasticSearch errors affecting search platform-wide, missing Learner Home Insight Cards impacting multiple clients, authentication failures at login, and a critical API server startup blocker. These high-severity issues suggest insufficient pre-release testing of core functionality.
Long-standing technical debt was addressed, including a 582-day-old CredSpark GAT assessment bug (dating to March 2024), a 147-day search filter issue, and a 137-day authentication redirect problem. However, 9 of 26 resolved bugs (35%) exceeded their SLA targets, indicating chronic backlog management challenges.

Trend Summary:
Strong fix velocity for a major release. 32 total bugs resolved against 35 opened gives a net of only +3 new bugs, same as the previous release — the best ratio since the 2025.4.3.0 cycle (+5) and a dramatic improvement over the 2025.4.1.0 spike (+42).  
Skills and Pathways dominated both sides of the ledger. Skills-related issues were the most common theme in both bugs opened (6 across Skill Ratings, Skill Standards, Skill Coach, and Skill Plans) and fixed (4). CCO delivered 7 fixes touching Pathways, Automations, Groups, and content management — a solid clearance of backlog items. 
ACM resolved 3 budget/virtual-card bugs — all P2s related to virtual card transactions and budget resets — indicating a focused sprint on financial workflow reliability. However, 2 of the 3 exceeded the 30-day SLA (52 and 63 days), so these lingered before being addressed. 
Aging P2/P3 backlog remains a concern. 6 of 11 P2s exceeded the 30-day SLA, including one at 399 days (In-App notification repeat issue, finally closed). Five P3s also exceeded SLA with ages ranging from 233 to 315 days, mostly in Skills+ and CCO. While closing these older items is positive, it shows that issues linger as P3 for too long and are only addressed once upgraded to P2.

Trend Summary:
Academies and Skills remain the two most persistent bug-generating feature areas this cycle. Six Academies bugs were resolved, all addressing core client-facing workflows (cloning, notifications, UI rendering), yet the Content Marketplace team still accounts for 10 of 25 newly opened bugs — a pattern unbroken since September 2025. Skills tells a similar story: 4 newly opened bugs, all P2 and client-reported, against only 2 resolved, meaning both feature areas are net-negative on backlog movement. Given that Skills is a core platform differentiator and Academies is central to many clients' learning delivery, the sustained inflow in both areas warrants more than sprint-by-sprint clearing — a focused stabilisation review of test coverage and regression patterns in each would be a worthwhile investment.
A P1 infrastructure incident is skewing the opened-bug picture significantly. A single P1 ElasticSearch degradation in US Production generated 22 Zendesk tickets and 20 Salesforce associations on its own — more than the entire resolved bug set combined in terms of support tickets. This one incident is responsible for a large portion of the inflated numbers this cycle. That said, search degradation is one of the most visible failures an end user can experience, and the breadth of client impact (20 affected organisations) makes a post-mortem and preventative investment worthwhile.
Net-new of +5 is among the better cycles historically, but not the best. The cycle immediately before this one — 2026.1.0.0 (Feb 10) — came in at +6, so this release represents a marginal improvement rather than a trend continuation. The broader picture shows the team has meaningfully pulled back from the worst periods (Nov 11 2025.4.1.0 at +42, Oct 14 2025.3.5.0 at +22), and is now operating in a much more stable range, which is a positive directional signal even if net-new has not yet turned negative.
SLA debt on older bugs is the quiet concern this cycle. While P2 compliance is solid at 3 of 4 within SLA, four P3 bugs resolved this cycle had been open between 133 and 288 days — nearly 10 months in the most extreme case. These are bugs that slipped through multiple prior cycles without resolution, and their eventual closure is welcome, but it raises the question of how many similar long-tail items remain in the backlog. The 92-day P2 breach (3x the 30-day SLA) is also worth flagging: proactively identifying and triaging the oldest open P2s and P3s before they breach would be a healthier posture than resolving them reactively.

Trend Summary:
Net backlog reduction — a positive reversal. This cycle delivered a net reduction of 4 bugs (20 solved vs. 16 opened), which is a notable improvement compared to recent cycles that consistently added to the backlog. This is the first cycle in several releases where the team resolved more client-reported bugs than were logged.
Content Marketplace dominated the resolved work. The ACM team resolved 9 bugs this cycle, all related to Academies: section reports displaying incorrectly, navigation issues, email duplication, user sync failures, and content visibility problems. The volume and breadth of these fixes signal sustained, focused effort on stabilizing the Academies product area.
Skills remains a recurring concern area. Three new Skills bugs appeared, continuing a multi-cycle pattern seen since at least the 2025.3.3.0 release. Two new SAP BTP integration bugs were also logged this cycle, pointing to potential instability in that connector.
P2 SLA performance remains a critical concern. Six of eight P2 Major bugs (75%) were resolved beyond their 30-day target, with resolution times ranging from 52 to 281 days. While 9 of 12 P3 bugs met their 120-day SLA, the persistent P2 overruns — several exceeding 150+ days — represent ongoing technical debt and continued risk to client trust and service commitments.

Trend Summary:
Academies stabilisation takes centre stage — and delivers. The Content Marketplace (ACM) team resolved 10 Academies-related bugs this cycle, covering video playback failures, cohort group membership sync issues, welcome email incorrectness, blank session screens, and a critical P1 launch failure. This is the highest ACM resolution count in the last four cycles and signals a focused, sustained effort to stabilise the Academies product area after several cycles of high inflow. Combined with the LXP team's 17 fixes, this cycle achieved a net reduction of 5 bugs in the backlog — only the second net-negative result in the past twelve releases and an improvement on the -4 recorded in the previous cycle (2026.1.2.0).
CCO and Integrations dominate the newly opened backlog. Of 22 newly logged bugs, 7 belong to CCO (covering Pathway completions, Credly badge issuance, Google Books API quota errors, and tech admin permission failures) and 6 to Content Marketplace (Academy progress tracking, notification emails, and custom domain redirects). The Integrations team continues to contribute recurring theme bugs around skill mapping and data collectors. The concentration of new issues in CCO and Content Marketplace — both high-visibility areas — warrants prioritised triage to prevent carryover.
P2 SLA performance shows meaningful improvement but long-tail debt persists. Seven of nine P2 Major bugs (78%) were resolved within the 30-day SLA this cycle — the strongest P2 compliance rate in at least the last five releases. However, two legacy P2s required 93 and 425 days to close, the latter representing over 14 months of open time and a significant historical debt item. On the P3 side, 13 of 17 (76%) met the 120-day target, but four exceeded it by 8 to 173 days. While directional trends are positive, these outliers reinforce that proactive triage of aging items remains an open discipline gap.
DevOps and platform stability surface as an emerging concern. A P1 file-processing bug was logged involving long processing delays or failures, generating the highest single-bug Zendesk ticket volume of any opened issue this cycle. Additionally, a custom logo branding outage and intermittent content import failures point to infrastructure-level fragility. The DS/AI team also logged a Maestro user-flow regression. While none of these individually constitute a systemic failure, their combined appearance in a single cycle suggests platform infrastructure deserves a focused stability review ahead of upcoming major releases.

Trend Summary:
A significant spike in bugs opened this cycle. At 31 bugs opened against only 16 resolved, this cycle produced the highest net-new count (+15) in recent releases, sharply reversing the negative net-new trends of the two prior cycles (-4 and -5). The newly opened bugs were spread widely across Skills, CCO, Learner Experience, Reporting, and Integrations, with no single team accounting for a dominant share — suggesting this is broad-surface noise rather than one problematic area, though it will need close monitoring going into the next cycle. 
Academies had a productive but bug-heavy fortnight. All Academies bugs resolved this cycle were end-user facing, covering cohort welcome email failures, enrollment redirect issues, content rendering problems, and contributor notification spam — a positive sign of active stabilization. However, two fresh Academies bugs also appear in the opened list this cycle (academy completion status and content descriptions).
LXP fixes were spread across nine teams with strong P3 SLA performance. The 12 LXP bugs resolved spanned areas including Pathways, the External API, Skills, Platform Services, and Mobile — reflecting healthy cross-team throughput. All eight P3 bugs resolved this cycle were closed well within the 120-day SLA (2-58 days), which is a strong result and consistent with recent cycles.
P2 SLA figures should be interpreted with caution. Four of the eight P2 bugs exceeded the 30-day SLA, with resolution times ranging from 32 to 128 days. This is a known pattern: many of these bugs are initially logged as P3 and only escalated to P2 after client pressure, meaning the SLA clock effectively starts late relative to the true severity timeline. The more meaningful signal is that these long-standing escalated issues were ultimately resolved this cycle, which is a net positive.

Trend Summary:
Lower bug-fix volume is consistent with a Major Release focus. At 14 total bugs solved, this is the lowest fix count in the past six months — but that's expected. Major Releases (*.5.0) are feature-delivery milestones where engineering bandwidth is weighted toward new functionality rather than bug clearance, as was similarly seen in the previous MPRs.
Net new bugs are elevated for a second consecutive cycle. With 24 bugs opened and 14 closed, the net new count of +10 follows last release's +15 (2026.1.4.0), marking two back-to-back cycles in positive territory after three cycles where bugs closed had matched or outpaced bugs opened. This suggests new features introduced over recent sprints may be generating a sustained tail of regressions worth tracking.
Skills and Learner Experience are the hotspots in the opened bugs. These two teams account for 14 of the 24 bugs opened (58%), with themes clustering around onboarding flows (recommended skills not showing, role removal issues, resume upload links), the My Team/Skill Coach experience, and general Learner Experience UI issues. This is particularly worth watching given that Skills-related functionality is a significant part of what was shipped in this release.
ACM responsiveness remains strong, and P2 SLA breaches were marginal. All four ACM bugs counted this cycle were resolved within 3 days of being logged — a positive signal of team agility. The two P2 breaches (35 and 39 days against a 30-day SLA) were only slightly over the threshold.

Trend Summary:
Net new bugs hold at +3 — a clear, sustained improvement after two elevated cycles. With 22 bugs opened against 19 resolved (16 LXP + 3 ACM), this cycle continues the directional recovery from the previous two releases (+10 in 2026.1.5.0 and +15 in 2026.1.4.0). After the post-MPR regression tail flagged last cycle, this is a meaningful sign that the new functionality is starting to stabilise rather than continuing to generate inflow.
Maestro and DS/AI emerge as a new concentration in the opened backlog. Four of the 22 opened bugs touch Maestro Studio, Maestro Roleplays, or audience-group behaviour, split across the Data Science and DS/AI teams — three of them P2 Major. This is the first cycle in recent memory where Maestro-related issues form a distinct theme rather than appearing as isolated tickets, and it warrants close attention given Maestro's growing role in the AI-powered Degreed experience.
CCO and Pathways saw both strong closure and renewed inflow. CCO resolved two well-targeted bugs this cycle (trackable links and the pathway-delete error redirect), but four new CCO bugs were also logged — covering pathway author permissions, pathway followers visibility, content card layout, and a security-relevant pathway-delete permission gap. The pathway-delete permission issue (P2) is particularly worth flagging as a regression risk, since it allows collaborators to delete pathways without the required permission. ACM responsiveness on Academies cohort issues remains solid, with all three counted ACM fixes closing within 42–57 days.
SLA performance is healthy on P2s but shows two long-tail P3 outliers. Four of six P2 bugs (67%) resolved within the 30-day SLA — the two breaches at 35 and 45 days were only marginally over. P3 compliance is strong at 11 of 13 (85%) within the 120-day SLA, with most closing well under 60 days. However, two P3s required 139 and 250 days to resolve (a weekly-digest email failure and a DDC content-catalog skills-missing issue), both representing legacy backlog items.
Hotfix activity was exceptionally low at just 1 —  the lowest in the last 15 cycles (average ~5.5), a strong signal that 2026.2.0.0 shipped cleanly.

Trend Summary:
Reporting was the dominant theme this cycle, accounting for the largest share of fixes (5 bugs) and a recurring source of newly logged issues spanning In-App report inaccuracies, permission-role errors, and content classification inconsistencies; the subsystem remains a high-touch area worth proactive hardening.
On the positive side, throughput on aging tickets was strong: the team cleared long-standing P3 issues (one open 294 days, another 109), 10 of 11 P3 fixes landed within the 120-day SLA, and the lone P1 was resolved same-day.
Critically, net new bugs were positive (+6) and Salesforce associations on opened bugs (51) far outpaced those on solved bugs (24), indicating broad client exposure on newly surfaced defects, with External API (4), Integrations (3) and Reporting (3) leading incoming reports.
Content Marketplace (Academies) shows a slow-resolution pattern: only 2 ACM bugs closed inside the window while several Major items clustered just outside it, and the two P2 SLA breaches (34 and 132 days) suggest Major-severity items are not consistently meeting the 30-day target.
